turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: We will hear argument this morning in Case 24-1063, Hunter versus United States. Ms. Blatt.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: All contracts are subject to defenses. So appeal waivers are also subject to defenses and can be excused in more circumstances than the two recognized below. The government argues that no contract defense can apply to knowing and voluntary appeal waivers, no exceptions. But, if appeal waivers bind defendants under contract principles, they can be excused under contract principles. A contrary approach is anathema to our criminal justice system. No appeal for sentences above the statutory maximum based on race, religion, national origin, tribal status, or pure vindictiveness. No appeal for pregnancy bans, castration, or compelled church attendance either. These scenarios are all real cases. Or take this case. Only the most paramount government interest overcomes the fundamental right to refuse medical treatment. But Hunter was sentenced to a mandatory medical condition even though he had no mental health issues, let alone one connected to the crime or related to any sentencing goal. Defendants bargain for caps on their sentencing exposure. Those bargains are destroyed by plainly unlawful sentences that are totally outside the realm of plausibility of either party. Now floodgates concerns are belied by decades of experience in the 11 circuits that reject the Fifth Circuit's rule. Applying defenses to appeal waivers maintains the status quo. Contract defenses are narrow by design and rarely satisfied. Appeal waivers will continue to bar the vast majority of alleged sentencing errors, guidelines miscalculations, insufficient explanation, or misbalancing under 3553(a). But contract defenses may be satisfied in the rare case of clear legal error and punishment unauthorized by statute or that violates the Constitution. At a minimum, this Court should remand on the second question presented. I welcome questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Ms. Blatt, if you can waive constitutional rights or statutory rights, why can't you waive these contractually rights that are statutory here?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: You absolutely can waive the -- the -- the right to appeal and there's a appeal agreement, but the issue here is, absent this plea agreement, which is a contract, the defendant could just walk away. The only thing that is binding him to the waiver, and the government is asserting, the enforceability of the appeal waiver by contract. I don't think there's any dispute that the plea agreement is a contract. If you waive your right to trial when you -- at -- at the arraignment and just say I waive my fundamental right to trial, that's a pretty big waiver. Tomorrow, you can get up and say, you know what, Judge, I think I want a jury trial. You can just revoke it. But, here, there's a plea agreement. There's a contract. The government has to rely on the contract principles. The government gets out of plea agreements by relying on contract defenses. And there's just no rational -- or it's just inexplicable that you would not apply contract defenses to this contract when you do to all others.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Could -- in this case, could the government simply void the agreement and proceed with the 10 counts?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah, I -- I don't think so, but here's why. The government has in many cases asserted frustration of purpose and did exactly what you said. I think, here, because the government has no interest and is not defending the mandatory medication condition, there's just no countervailing principles. Now the government has not disputed it can allege a breach of the plea agreement, and that would result in an invalidation of the plea. The government just doesn't dispute that. That's why defendants so rarely bring these appeals, is because they run a substantial risk of exactly what you're saying. We would try to argue that there's no breach based on the fact that the judge told him he had a right to appeal. And even if it is a breach, you know, it's -- it's justified by defending the contract exception, and the government doesn't have any countervailing interest to invalidate the whole agreement. And that's all the stuff we would fight on remand. Here, we're cut off because the -- the Fifth Circuit has this categorical rule that, although it's a contract and you're denied the benefit of the bargain with sentences that exceed the statutory maximum, it allows no other exceptions.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Counsel, it seems to me that you're really asking for an equivalent of a Miranda warning that somebody would have to give, the -- the prosecutor, maybe even the judge, going through all the things that you're required to. What -- what exactly -- what are the elements of what they must -- you must tell the -- the defendant so long as you -- so that you don't leave any, you know, strings untied?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So there's a distinction between the first question presented and the second question presented. If the judge said absolutely nothing, we'd still be here on a contract defense. There's no warnings. It's just there's 30 years of -- actually, 34 years where this has been the law. We think, in terms of the second question presented, all the judge has to say is that you had -- you may have a right to appeal; you should look at your plea agreement. That's it. There's no other warnings. There's no nothing. So that's the second question presented, but on the first question presented, we're just arguing there's -- the government is enforcing the plea waiver. We're saying it's subject to one of contract defenses or a miscarriage-of-justice exception. So that's all to brief on remand. This has nothing to do with what a court would have to -- to --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- educate the defendant about.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Well, I think it does have to do with that, and it seems to me that the court is the only one can do -- that can do it. You know, if you're the defense attorney, I don't know, you want to advise your client, of course, but, you know, if the judge says something that's not right, it seems to me that it's -- the lawyer's in a tough position because the judge has given them an opportunity to raise this after the fact if you think -- and so it would be odd, I think, for the lawyer to stand up and say, oh, Your Honor, remember you've got to tell him that he can't do this or this or, otherwise, it might not be valid.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So, on the second question presented, we're literally arguing a straight litigation forfeiture waiver. All we're saying -- this is on the second question presented -- all courts hold what you just said except for the Ninth Circuit. It doesn't matter what the judge says, except the Ninth Circuit. What we're trying to argue on the second question presented is, when the judge said you have a right to appeal, given the back and forth on the medication condition, and when the prosecutor said, Your Honor, I believe, um -- well, I -- no, that he -- two things happened. One, the defendant detrimentally relied on that in bringing the appeal and exposed himself to a breach, and, two, and I think the better argument, is, had the prosecutor spoken up, we would not be wondering had the court imposed this bizarre medication condition thinking that there could be a right to appeal, and that's a simple remand.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Well, that's kind of --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: But the more important question is the first question presented, where all 11 circuits -- this has nothing to do with what -- what a court would advise. You just sign a plea agreement, and there are broad waivers and that's it. Defendants don't bring these because it's an alleged breach. You do have these rare exceptions where it can mean a contract defense or be a miscarriage of justice, the castration example, a pregnancy ban, the government's view, race, mandatory medication that tough, that -- that's it, you signed the appeal waiver. That's absurd when everyone from Elon Musk to billionaires to corporations to mom-and-pop -- mom-and-pops can assert contract defenses. The government doesn't even tell you why this contract, unlike any other contract in the entire universe, can't have a defense.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Ms. -- Ms. Blatt, I guess I'm trying to figure out what the nature of your argument is. You refer to contract defenses. Then you say or a miscarriage-of- justice exception. In the time you've been up there, you've said the question is whether it's outside the realm of plausibility. You've referred to constitutional violations.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I mean, when is it that this would come into play in your view more specifically?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So, hopefully, almost never because courts don't do crazy things like here, but let me --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, but I'm asking for a standard.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Like, what are we looking to?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: The -- the -- the standard is that the easiest and the high-level rule statement is it's subject to contract defenses.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, I don't -- what --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So now I'm going to --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- what is a contract defense? Does that just mean it's against public policy or --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: No, I'm going to break that --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- are you referring to other kinds of --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- break that down. There are hundreds of years of precedent on very narrow defenses that sound maybe in label broad, but once you take off that label, they all have a thumb on the scale, exceedingly a thumb on the scale in favor of enforcement. That's why contract defenses are so rare. And if you go past the Restatements, you start reading why they're never applied, including public policy. Public policy, as this Court said in Rumery, the first public policy is enforcement of the contract. So you would have to have some really out there public policy that would outweigh that now.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Is there -- is there anything other that you're -- than public policy that you're talking about --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: No. I think --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- when you refer to contract defenses?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- I think the one that makes -- public policy's a good one because it's rooted in this Court's case law. The one that makes the most sense is the one the government asserts, which is frustration of purpose. The government has gotten out of plea agreements in the two cases both in the Fifth Circuit and the Tenth Circuit because they said it was frustrated when a defendant didn't do anything wrong but just got out on collateral relief. Just -- so if I -- frustration of purpose, why I think it's the best and that's why I got the standard of outside the realm of plausibility.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Ms. Blatt --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, Ms. Blatt, I -- I think you've got a strong argument on something like miscarriage of justice or something that's shockingly improper, something that would capture the examples that you started out with, where the -- the sentence is based on race or religion or something like that. I think you've got a -- a strong argument on that. When you go further, then I get nervous because public policy is very broad. Plea agreements have some attributes of a contract, but they're not just -- they're not like ordinary contracts. And the -- imposing the entire law of contracts here seems to me rather open-ended. So --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Okay. Then can you please say the entire law of contracts does not apply to the government? Because that's all they do is cite contract law in enforcing these plea agreements. So make sure you write your decision that they're not enforceable either under contract principles, that the government can't rely on it. It is a double standard, including in the Fifth Circuit.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: All right. Well, what do you in concrete terms, what do you think would -- the law of contracts would say about a variety of issues that might be waived? How about constitutional venue?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I -- I don't know what you mean by that.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Sixth Amendment venue.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So all I'm talking --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: The right to be tried in the --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: -- the state or district where the offense occurred.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- I mean, waiver, Mezzanatto, you have a million cases on waiver. In terms of -- this case involves no waiver of a trial right. This is involving a waiver of the right to appeal sentencing errors. And all -- the courts have generally said miscarriage of justice, but all of -- at least eight of them have said contract principles.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: What about the Sixth Amendment right to a speedy trial?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Well, you have -- you have cases on all of this about the waiver has to be knowing and voluntary and then you have to decide if it's a personal right versus a public right.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: What about the suppression of evidence under the Fourth Amendment or the Sixth Amendment?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: You waive all that when you waive your right to trial. A defendant can almost never get out of a plea agreement. You've held back in, I think, '72 under Brady that plea agreements are binding and they waive the right. The only way to get out of a plea agreement --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: So I -- I thought -- I thought the only thing --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- you have cases on that, Lafler. You actually have Supreme Court cases that say you can almost never get out of a plea agreement.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Ms. Blatt, I thought the only thing before us was waiver of sentencing errors.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Not anything else.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Correct. Because you have a million cases saying defendants can't get out of plea agreements.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah. And on -- on -- on -- on Justice Alito's point about whether these are ordinary contracts or not, if they're not, we might think about unconstitutional conditions doctrine in this area too. And I was a little surprised we didn't have more of that in the briefing before us, and I'm kind of curious why. I mean, as I understand it, the defendant here pleaded guilty to one count of fraud for $32,000. That was the only amount charged in the indictment. And then he wound up being sentenced for something like a half a million dollars worth of fraud and a whole bunch of other unindicted incidents. And it's not clear to me what benefit he got from his plea waiver -- appeal waiver, I should say, that would be proportionate and have a nexus to that additional right foregone.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So let me just -- and I think this captures, Justice Alito, a lot of defendants are unhappy with their sentences, but when they're -- and they're mostly guidelines, but within -- when they're within the statutory maximum, courts have universally said tough luck, that part of the appeal waiver is the assumption that the court will make ordinary errors in sentencing guidelines as long as you're sentenced within the statutory maximum. The government's view is you can sentence someone for 30 years for a misdemeanor and there's no right to defense. Now they want to try to rely on maybe something in the contract, but that contract provision doesn't say what the government says. So -- nor the Fifth Circuit. The Fifth Circuit, I mean --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: With respect, Ms. Blatt, do I get an answer to my question about why we don't have an unconstitutional condition?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: It -- the -- the sentence seemed to me, 51 months and all the fines, I -- I don't think he -- because we're just -- I mean, yeah, the defendant's not happy with his sentence. Most defendants aren't. But they're within the statutory maximum. So I think that's the answer. The condition of probation -- sorry, the condition of supervised release is the preposterous one that says you have to follow the recommendations.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: In your --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: One -- one might argue, though, that that's an unconstitutional condition to be required to give up a bodily -- a right of bodily integrity without a compelling showing by the government.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Or any showing.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Any showing.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I mean, it's just totally bizarre. The government doesn't defend it. It's -- I'm going to get this example out. If there was a law that was passed that said all judges must take antidepressants recommended by their physician, I think you would find it stupidly unconstitutional and offensive.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, I think we already have. I mean, we've got a very --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: And it might be medically -- and it might be medically appropriate to take antidepressants.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- 50-year-old case --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: It doesn't matter.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But, Ms. -- Ms. Blatt, is the problem that you couldn't really raise Justice Gorsuch's very significant point because there is this waiver? Like, the unconstitutional condition, I think, is an argument that could be made in the context of an appeal when you were trying to say that the -- that the trial judge or the sentencing judge did something wrong.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yes. And --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But the problem is you have a waiver, so you never even get to raise that issue.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Can't raise it. That's what I was trying to say to Justice Kagan. In the universe of reading all of these cases, the court of -- so, first of all, defendants don't bring them because they don't want to get turned around and have the plea agreement breached. The lawyers don't want to bring them because they've been sanctioned. And, third, the court of appeals don't want to see these cases because they have to deal with it. And that's why, when you read them, they say, this is going to be rarely applied, please don't bring them, it has to be an extreme case.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So is your issue --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, it might be rare, but, usually, you know, we have a standard, you know, what are those rare cases? How do we identify them? And that's what I'm trying to figure out.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Now you said, well, you could definitely argue if it's above the statutory maximum. But the Fifth Circuit actually seems to agree with you. So -- so, on that one, you're in the same place as the Fifth Circuit. So what else? And how do we identify these errors? And is it -- I guess maybe the backup point is, is this really a place where we should look to contract law, or is it a place where we should just say, yes, we should have an exception for, you know, miscarriages of justice and -- and people know what that means --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- and know that --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. And --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- it refers only to extreme cases?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: And what I've distilled, which I think is a really good standard, is, first, the error has to be obvious, like clear and indisputable, like no even argument on the other side. It just has to be a really bad mistake, whether that's in excess of the statutory maximum, the case involving Judge Stras in the Eighth Circuit, it was a person who possessed a gun. The court imposed a very bizarre, unrelated, weird condition that he had a lifetime ban. It just kind of came out of nowhere. Those are clear errors. Now, once you get past clear error, like, if you're having an argument about it, the defendant is just going to lose. But, when it's a stupid clear error, that's one. That's the first gateway. The second gateway is exactly what you said. It's got to be really bad. And what I said what really bad means is it's just so plainly unauthorized by statute or a violation of a constitutional right, which covers race, First Amendment, religion, bodily integrity.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Why --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But there is another option, isn't there, I mean, in addition to those possibilities for saying this waiver is unenforceable? Isn't there the option of saying that we really just don't allow these kinds of waivers? In other words, that, you know, it's not, for example, knowing and voluntary for a defendant to prospectively waive his right to object or to appeal a sentencing error.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And so they're just not enforceable. I know you don't make this argument --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: No, but here's --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- but there are -- there are --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: No, I like it that you have a case kind of against me on it. So the -- the knowing and voluntary just says you have to know, you know, you know all about what the waiver of the right to mean. You don't have to know the consequences. So that's why. Now there are some --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Well, I understand, but there are -- there are district court judges, very thoughtful district court judges, who have accepted the view that this is different than a trial waiver that, you know, goes with your guilty plea --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- that there's something unique about the prospective waiver of a sentencing proceeding that you know is going to happen, but you're giving up your right to appeal any errors with respect to that proceeding, that -- that that's just -- you can't knowingly --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- have that kind of waiver some courts have said.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- you're correct -- the Fourth Circuit in 2025 in a -- in a case, Smith, cited by Cato, does exactly what you said. It said this cannot possibly be a knowing and voluntary waiver, but we're going to also apply miscarriage of justice. So they did the two steps, but they said what you said. I just don't know if the rest of you would agree with that analysis, but they said --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Ms. -- Ms. Blatt --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What -- what are the --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- exactly what you said.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- what are the specific exceptions that you would identify? Ineffective assistance of counsel?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So that, yeah, goes to knowing and voluntary.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: And then --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And a sentence exceeds the statutory maximum, right?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And unconstitutional factors used in the sentence?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. I would just call that violates the Constitution. Now --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, those are three.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: No, just two.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, I listed three there, but --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Hold on. Let me just -- can I combine them into two? So the unauthorized by statute, the problem with the Fifth Circuit is, you know, you could have what that means. You could have restitution unauthorized by statute. In the case of the Eighth Circuit, they applied a lifetime ban on certain things for this gun. It was -- just unauthorized by the statute to me covers a little more than sentence -- sentences exceeding the maximum. Also, this condition of supervised release, if it was you have to go to church, it's just -- it's not allowed by statute. It's just -- and it's plainly unconstitutional.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, supervised release conditions, I think, do present a different bucket, and I just wonder --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: They do.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- how would you characterize what the exception --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Oh. Are just --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- for conditions of supervised --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- unconstitutional. You can could say it exceeds the statute. The statute is very broad about reasonably necessary. Yeah, it's just got a lot of, you know, yada, yada, yada about kinds of stuff. But, when it gets to this kind of stuff, which is just weird stuff like, you know, go to church -- castration seems a little bit outside the statute. One of them said the defendant was getting so many women pregnant out of wedlock -- this is a federal court -- and said, if you're going to get someone pregnant, can you please make it your wife? You know, you just can't do that. Come on, that's silly.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Counsel, how much of this --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: It's just -- you just can't do that. And that's unauthorized by the supervised release statute.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And then anything beyond that? I just want to have a defined list if we're going to do this.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So what cabins it is the first part. You can't just say, well, I have -- you know, I don't want to talk to my probation officer every day because that violates my free speech. You're not even going to get past the first gate, I mean, and you're going to run the risk of getting sanctions. So all that kind of garbage is out.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Ms. Blatt --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And then you -- if I could finish.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah. Please.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Sorry.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: On your -- in your beginning, you said clear legal error. And now that seems to open up -- because everyone thinks their legal error is clear, open up a wider box. And I just want to make sure --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So, no, because you can say, like, mandamus, clear and indisputable, clearly established, plain error, the error has to be obvious, clear. And that's what the courts below say. You can even say it louder. I mean, you -- you get -- you get to say what you want.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, some courts say that and some courts don't. There's -- that's why we're here. There's a split.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Some -- I will be honest, some are really broad and just say any illegal sentence. And guess what, there's no flood, there's no appeals. There's 30 years of this. The first --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I'm not disputing you.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Okay. Okay. I'm sorry.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: These are not hostile questions.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So -- okay. The thing is I -- I trust the system on the three systemic reasons --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- because there's an appeal waiver.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: And the court of appeals don't want to see these, and lawyers, you know, are very careful in bringing them. On terms of clear error, I do think it's like the second prong of Olano. It's just plain error. It's easy. It's applied every day in forfeiture. No one has a problem misunderstanding that.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Are you essentially endorsing the Fourth Circuit's test, which is a fundamental constitutional or statutory right that was firmly established at the time of sentencing? Is that what you're saying to Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I like that. I mean, yeah, that sounds good. I hadn't heard that one, but, yeah, I just think in terms of Justice --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Fourth Circuit's -- that's the Fourth Circuit's standard.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I'm just trying to get Justice Alito's vote, and what I'm trying to say --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- to Justice Alito is -- I care about the rest of you too, but --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Thank you very much.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: It's very -- very few advocates have that --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: I'll just stop then.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: -- goal.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Ms. Blatt --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Sorry.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- I'm -- I'm trying to figure out how we articulate this, okay? You've come in with a simple proposition that ordinary contract principles, defenses should generally apply. There might be some --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: The same standard the government has.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The same standard the government has. And that's not just frustration of purpose because frustration of purpose means both a procedural and substantive component. And you may not have a procedural component in this kind of waiver. But I think the other defense are policy, public policy, it's against public policy. That's the rationale for statutory maximums, which is Congress has said you should only receive a sentence of X amount, okay? That's what you get.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. And there's separation of powers issues. Yeah.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Exactly. Unconscionability.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I think it depends on your comfort level with those two words.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. But I think --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: A lot of people freak out at that word. I don't because --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I know, but I don't either.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And the implied duty of good faith, that has to do with a judge doing what he's supposed to do, and that subsumes unconscionability -- that subsumes --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yes, and --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- clear error.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yes, and --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It subsumes constitutional error.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: And is a heavy thumb on the -- the implied -- parties' expectation that the contract would be enforced. So what I think what the government -- the scare tactics, they sound really broad. They're not because, if you just read one Delaware case, it'll tell you the implied covenant of good faith has to mean --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. Let me -- let me just stop you on all of this discussion.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Arizona, Minnesota, California, and Texas, all of their high courts say that they -- a prospective waiver of a not-yet-imposed sentence is not enforceable, all right, when the defendant does not know what errors, if any, that there could be because they're not giving up a right --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: A -- a known --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- a known right. You haven't argued that. It may be too much water under the bridge, but there is a strong argument, by the way, and it -- and it goes back to something that Justice -- that Justice Gorsuch was referring to -- on the sentencing guidelines. The reports when the guidelines came out said we're permitting appeal waivers because appellate review of sentences is essential to ensure that the guidelines are -- are applied properly and to provide case law development -- development of the reasons for sentencing outside the guidelines.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. Guideline errors are just going to be out. And they've been here for --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I know. But -- but don't give up so easily, okay?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Let me just break it down and --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, no, no, let me just come down to --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Oh, sure. Yeah, take it.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Let me finish my question, okay?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. I'm sorry.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I know you want to correct me along the way, but --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: No, you're -- it's wonderful what you're saying, so keep going.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The reason why Justice Alito's arguments about trial errors doesn't work is because you are giving up some known defenses to a trial. You can look at all of those things, you can imagine them, and you can say whatever they are, I'm going to give them up. So those are never considered, the trial-related errors. What we're talking about is sentencing errors, which is at a time where you don't know what the judge is going to do and so you have to make certain presumptions. One of them is they're not going to act outside of their constitutional or statutory duty. That's your argument, isn't it?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. I -- I can tell you I did think about what you said because you can make an argument, like, in all your cases -- Mezzanatto is the best one that explains what you can waive -- and, basically, personal rights -- this goes to Justice Alito -- personal rights can be waived. Now you get to a certain point where there's a public interest in the actual right. And what we were trying to say when courts go so outside the bounds of what anyone could have been thinking, government can't even defend it, it's implausible, there's something to be said for the criminal justice system not bringing itself into disrepute and having these embarrassing sentences out there. And the judiciary has certainly the authority or duty to correct them. So you can --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- analyze it under Mezzanatto, but it just seemed like a higher -- higher hill to climb.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Just one brief question. How much of this is -- could be taken care of under the one ground that everybody seems to agree isn't waived, which is ineffective assistance of counsel? In other words, if you weren't advised by your counsel as -- that this is what could happen if I say yes, go ahead, you know, I -- I agree with the -- you know, to -- to waive -- allow the court to sentence as part of the presentence -- the agreement.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Because there's -- there's no fiduciary duty to say, and it's not just within the realm of plausible, ineffective assistance of counsel is you're waiving the right to appeal. I don't -- you would have to say, and I just don't think it makes sense, is that I have a fiduciary duty to tell you this judge may impose the death penalty and order castration. That's just not --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Well, that's why I said --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: -- that's why I said in many cases, not -- not all of them. I mean, for -- I don't know, for example -- maybe this is a good example, where the -- the counsel presumably knows a little bit about his client and he could advise him, look, you have to understand that if you accept this agreement, they may require you to take medication. And if he doesn't, then maybe that's something that can be brought --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: But that --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: -- as an ineffective assistance --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: With all due respect --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Maybe I'll finish the sentence.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. Okay, sorry.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: You know, if it doesn't, maybe that's something that could be brought as an ineffective assistance claim that would allow him to get out of the sentence.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I don't think that would succeed because it was not ineffective and this guy doesn't -- there was no mental health condition. He did it for the assistance of -- the probation officer just said it might be easier if this guy were medicated. That's preposterous. I mean, it might be easier if you were medicated too for my job. It just -- that's not how you get to medicate people.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Okay. Thank you. Justice Thomas? Justice Alito?

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, let me begin with Justice Sotomayor's rebuttal of what she took me to be asking about regarding constitutional rights. Now she will have the right to surrebuttal, which I won't have a chance to answer under this questioning regime that we have now. The Fourth Circuit test, which you seem to agree with, as I understand it, was any important constitutional right. So that wouldn't be limited to sentencing errors, would it? It would encompass all of the things I was asking about before.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: No. We're just talking about the appellate right on the sentence.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Okay. We're just talking about the sentence.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. That's all -- every circuit, the whole circuit split is about when you can appeal sentences. So, as Justice Jackson said, these are all things you -- you know, you -- you wouldn't --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: So anything that couldn't -- wasn't known prior to the time of sentence.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: At sentencing. Again, I tried to say with Justice Gorsuch it has to be clear. So, when you have an unconstitutional right to not go to church, and just it seems ridiculous that a -- a lawyer would say, by the way, you're sentencing this, I know you're religious, so there might be a chance at supervised release he's going to think you're a religious fanatic and say don't go to church. And all we're saying is come on.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Okay. What if it's a sentencing issue that was known by the parties before entering into the plea agreement?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. So all of that would be factored into any contract defense. It would be factored into miscarriage of justice. That's why guidelines are all out, is because it's known at the time of sentencing that, you know, you could be sentenced up to the statutory maximum. It really doesn't matter what the guidelines say.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: What about guidelines issues during the period when compliance with the guidelines was mandatory?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Oh, I think that that would have been -- I mean, now you're taking me back to --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: That's a sentencing error.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah, you're taking me back to pre-Booker. So, no, I don't -- I --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: No?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I don't think so. Sentencing guidelines is just something that happened. Now we could talk --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: I mean, it was above the statutory maximum because the statutory maximum was defined by the mandatory guidelines.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. And we -- look, we could talk about that, but we don't need to because it's advisory and all courts have said sentencing errors are out. And if you write this opinion, you can say that too. So that way you just --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: What if the guidelines call for a sentence of six months' imprisonment, but the -- but the judge says that's woefully inadequate and anyway, they're not mandatory, so --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Thirty years.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Right, right.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: The statutory maximum? Some of you could leave open the miscarriage of justice, but I just wouldn't because courts -- courts have just said they don't want to see sentencing errors, but, yeah, sure. If you want to say they -- they so misapplied the -- the criminal, whatever it's called, the -- the criminal career offender one where you get this massive bump, I just -- courts really don't want to see those. They just want, look, up to the statutory maximum, guideline errors are out.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Okay. One final question. If we're -- well, maybe not the final one. But, if we're going to apply contract law, what body of contract law do we -- do we look to? It's not the same everywhere.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. You look to the same that you would look every time you apply contract law, including in Rumery. You look to the Restatements, you look to centuries of case law.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: We look to the Restatements?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: That's what you did in Rumery. You cited Restatement 171, and that's what all the courts have done. I mean, you have -- the Court said -- held we'll apply, you know, general common law to contracts. But you have so many cases and plenty of collective bargaining agreements, we cite a lot of cases in the brief where you're applying contract law. Remember, the government is always applying contract law. I don't know where they're citing the state that they're in or they're citing -- I don't -- I don't think the cases really care. All contract defenses are so narrowly circumscribed that -- and the court of appeals, if 34 years, not even a trickle? Much less a flood, a river.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor, anything further? Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Just to make sure on a few things. You are not arguing that appeal waivers are impermissible across the board, correct?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Absolutely not.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And if that were to be the case, presumably, Congress or the Rules Committee would have to do that.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. I mean, you'd have to go through the analysis Justice -- Justice Sotomayor said, and you already had that Brady case that says you can plea. So I think it's just not going to win.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And then, notwithstanding an appeal waiver, you can appeal a condition of supervised released if -- if you could fill in the blank, just to summarize, not --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah. Not with -- I mean, the easiest for me is just it was not within the realm of plausibility of either party. So I would take into account the government's expectation that supervised release is no fun, but you have these -- some of these conditions and we've talked about them. It can't include who you get pregnant with. That's just -- no.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But, obviously, you want a broader standard than that. Is there a phrase you would use for the broader standard?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Oh, yeah. Just violates the Constitution and it was not within the realm of plausibility, government has no justified expectation in the enforcement of that condition. I mean, all these conditions, the government would look ridiculous saying, well, yeah, no, we really need to do that.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And then, last, I just want to confirm this. You just said this to Justice Alito, that misapplying the guidelines, procedural or substantive unreasonableness would not be a basis.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Not under any contract defense or any -- like, any public policy or not miscarriage of justice because it's always within the reasonable expectation that the court could veer off from the guidelines and impose under the statutory maximum. And this is what the courts of appeals have held, so I'm just parroting what's already been held.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I -- I agree. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So there's a lot of intuitive appeal to the contract analogy, especially because it's used for the underlying plea agreement. One thing that concerns me, however, is that at least on this question of appeal waivers, I don't understand any of the courts of appeals to go all in on this contract analogy.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Fair.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: And it wasn't fleshed out below in the Fifth Circuit. And I think you've had a lot of questions today exploring how there could be difficulties with it. So it seems kind of risky to go all in on this contract thing.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So I guess three responses. One, as always, you're correct. It's fair. Only eight circuits have applied contract law to plea agreements generally. And they're not -- I think maybe only the Tenth Circuit and the Second Circuit has talked about this in terms of defense. So you're just a hundred percent correct. Number two, ironically, that is the Fifth Circuit. The Fifth Circuit's rationale for all of this is you can always appeal above the statutory maximum because the defendant didn't get the benefit of his bargain. I mean, that's too broad and it kind of -- it -- you know, it makes the Fifth Circuit's decision just not defensible. But they say over and over again that's not what the defendant bargained for. So we've already gone down that crazy, you know, open-ended route, and so I --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Well, but Judge Bibas wrote an opinion in the Third Circuit that located the statutory maximum thing and separation of powers and said you'd be going beyond the sentence. So just because the Fifth Circuit said this would be beyond reasonable expectations of the bargain doesn't mean that that is, you know, what we would have to say is the rationale for that exception if we accepted it.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I -- I think separation of powers is good. It's just, you know, for the Constitution, you have to get a little more tricky with why that -- you know, outside of Article III power for a supervisor -- you know, a supervised -- condition of supervised release, it's just absurd, unconstitutional.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Well, and -- and here's another question I have about the contract analogy. It doesn't seem to me like a lot of the exceptions that we're batting around really fall within contract defenses. It's almost like we're trying to shoehorn them in.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I disagree because I've argued two cases in the Delaware Supreme Court, and they're all about expectations of the parties, and one side usually wins as I expected the contract to be enforced as written. The other side has to really get out of it by saying it's so outside the realm of industry standard or so outside the realm that anyone could have thought of, that anyone could have perceived.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Yeah, but what about the other ones? Like, what's the analogy for miscarriage of justice or -- I mean, that -- that -- that is grounded, I think, in systemic concerns, maybe constitutional concerns.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: That reflects the fact that this isn't a pure contract between the prosecutor and the defendant, but the judge is involved because the judge signs off on it. But that doesn't map on.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: And I think the statutory maximum thing, you know, okay, well, maybe we can talk about it. I mean, nobody's really put it in those contract terms. Maybe the Fifth Circuit has gestured that way.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: And I think --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But then what -- what -- what -- let me just finish this part of it.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: What rationale would there be for us not taking every possible contract defense that could come along or limiting it to certain ones? I take it you would say no, any contract defense from the Restatement would be on the table.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Justice Barrett, with all due respect, whatever the government can do, the defendant should be able to do. The government doesn't get to just say I can only look at three principles of contract law in interpreting the contract and the defendant has a different rule. I mean, that's just -- just whatever Elon Musk could get, a criminal defendant has to be able to get under a contract.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. But -- but, Ms. Blatt -- and I haven't looked at all the cases in which the government is saying that or alleging breach. You know, I -- I -- I know Judge Easterbrook said that before and invited the government essentially to -- to allege that there was a breach when the defendant appealed. But, in all of these contract analogies, you're looking at the term itself and asking if the term itself is, say, unconscionable. And you're not arguing here that the term, that the waiver of the statutory right to appeal, that term is not void against public policy.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: It's not unconscionable. You're talking about one narrow circumstance, which is the application of that term. So it's not really like the contract itself.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Well, so, A, you're absolutely correct in terms of what Justice Kavanaugh said, but in every contract, again, over and over, we cite cases involving government contracts where a third party has to apply that provision, and there's just defenses that apply when the third party goes off the rails. But unconscionability, I think you're right, it's tricky. So the only way we would get there -- unconscionability I haven't seen applied. The only way you would get there is, in the government's view, this contract literally says you waive your right to appeal no matter what, no matter how many body parts they remove for you and no matter what the court sentences, no matter what the contract says. And all we're saying is, oh, come on, there's got to be a contract defense to that somewhere.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. Last question. This is just a factual one. As I understand the record, the district court said at sentencing when he objected to this medical condition, medical condition of supervised release --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: -- that, listen, if you go and get treatment and they do prescribe drugs that you have to take, talk to your probation officer, and if that's not fruitful, you can come back and see me. Did he go back to see the district court?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: No. He just got out of jail and he now is under an ordered mental health treatment program. Now there's something slightly comical about that because Mr. Hunter thinks he will go to hell and thinks God has told him he can't take drugs, which is why he doesn't drink. That conversation is going to be a little difficult with the judge: Okay, I really -- God tells me no, and whatever that medical manual is says it's indicated. I just don't see that conversation working out too well. But, if it does, I mean, I don't know what this goes to, whether you think it's not a violation. Again, if -- I'll get a really bad law if all women have to --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: I -- no, no, no, no. I mean, like, I just wanted to know. I wanted to clarify that factually --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: You are correct.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: -- that's what happened because I do think then that gives him some recourse to go back to someone in the judicial system to ask. So I just --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Not really because, if the court says no --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But the court didn't say no here. He didn't go back.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Correct. But there's -- again, he has a threat of imprisonment if he doesn't take the drug. I mean, that's bad.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. Thanks.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Jackson?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So, when I'm thinking about this, I am mindful of the narrowness of the question presented as you have set it forth. You say whether the only permissible exceptions to a general appeal waiver -- waiver are for claims of an ineffective assistance of counsel or that the sentence exceeds the statutory maximum.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I suppose we could answer no --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- to the extent that we see other exceptions that some of the other circuits have accepted and we think those should be allowed as well. This questioning that you're getting is, I think, an effort to say what -- we could offer more than that --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- what exactly are circuits supposed to be doing. And you point to contract principles, but I guess that doesn't necessarily have to be where we go with this, right?

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Correct. I mean, the question presented is the Fifth Circuit is obviously wrong, please reverse and let us try to get a defense, but -- because there was questioning about, well, what will that mean on remand. But, no, you just need to say the Fifth Circuit's rule is indefensible.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Yes. And -- and I guess let me just add -- add one thing or ask one thing about the conversation you had with Justice Kavanaugh. I guess I'm not sure that Congress or the Rules Committee would necessarily be the only body that could determine whether these kinds of waivers are categorically unenforceable. I would think that based on our case law and sort of standing principles about knowing and voluntary waivers that this Court could perhaps avoid the concerns about picking a particular set of waivers, which -- I mean -- excuse me -- exceptions, which ones, are they contract principles or whatever, if we could be, enough of us, persuaded that in a circumstance where the defendant is pleading guilty and we know a sentencing hearing will happen, it is not sufficiently knowing to have a waiver of any errors that are going to happen in that sentencing proceeding, that it's just not an enforceable thing consistent with our understanding of knowingness and voluntariness with respect to the exercise of one's ability to make a waiver.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: So, A, correct, and, B, that's the law that Congress passed in 1984, which is you could bring appeals. Appeal waivers didn't even become boilerplate until the late 2000s. So we had a world in which you could just appeal your sentence. There was no flood.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And it's hard to see really -- and I'll ask the --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: But I -- I'm not going to get any votes for this, so --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: No, I understand. You don't want to waste your time because you feel that it is not --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: No, it's fine. I love what you're saying. And -- and nothing bad would happen too.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: You feel it's not productive, but my job is to make sure that all of the issues are being taken into account when the Court decides what they're going to do.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Oh, I think you can get there.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And my point is -- my point is that when we're thinking about what a waiver is and we're trying to understand the circumstances and we're balancing the public policy issues on both sides, I'm going to ask the government, what are they gaining out of ensuring that people don't appeal obvious waivers -- obvious errors in their sentencing.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Like, what's the public policy interest in that.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: My --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So all of those things might lead one rationally to think it's really not worth the candle for us to try to parse out exactly what kinds of waivers in which situations we would allow or not allow as opposed to just saying we're not going to allow this sort of thing because of the knowingness and voluntariness problem.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: I mean, as I sit here, I kind of like what you're saying, and I -- I actually think it's a way of looking at it because nobody brings these appeals. I think, from the government, just putting the government hat on, they just don't like the guideline appeals because people get -- you know, they're advisory and if the court goes above it. But you could just -- you could get all the same way and say, well, that has to be knowing because everyone knows that the guidelines are advisory. Just in terms of the state of the law now, it's just not that bad for the government. They just don't --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Okay? Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Ms. Jacoby.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Knowing and voluntary presentence appeal waivers are enforceable bargains, and no doctrine of contract law calls for a different result based on the subsequent sentence that the defendant receives. Petitioner asks this Court to create new substantive limits on the enforceability of those knowing and voluntary waivers, but he's provided no administrable limits on which waivers should be in or out. The litigation that would inevitably follow under his front-line approach would essentially eliminate all appeal waivers. As a fallback, Petitioner proposes a safety valve to appeal waivers for the most egregious sentences. But, without more real-world evidence of the most farfetched hypotheticals, this is a solution in search of a problem, and it's a solution that comes with costs. An amorphous exception for egregious sentences will also invite litigation over what is egregious. Such litigation will deprive the government of the benefit of its bargain, thus making appeal waivers less valuable as bargaining chips for defendants. In all events, this Court should make clear that there is no exception that would permit Petitioner's appeal rather than simply remanding, as Petitioner suggests. No injustice would be worked by dismissing Petitioner's unripe due process claim, but remanding would signal down on that score to lower courts and litigants, opening the floodgates to garden-variety waived appeals like Petitioner's. Finally, this Court should reject Petitioner's argument as to the second question presented. The district judge's statement that Petitioner had a right to appeal was accurate under the terms of the waiver and circuit precedent, but even if it were not, the government's non-objection to a misstatement by the district court did not modify or waive the parties' written appeal waiver. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: If the -- if this Court says that the agreement is void, would a remedy that would eliminate the underlying agreement be that you totally eliminate the plea agreement?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes, I do think there is a possibility that if this is -- you know, this is an essential part of the bargain, and if we are not getting our part of the bargain, then we don't have to enforce our -- our end of the bargain either. I think Petitioner has argued that the possibility that we'll, you know, reinstate the dropped charges is enough of a disincentive to stop the floodgates of dismissed appeals, but -- sorry -- pardon -- of -- of waived appeals. But, in practice, that's not actually much of a deterrent because it's not some -- it's not an option we avail of our -- avail ourselves of all that often because our whole point in bargaining for an appeal waiver is we want to bring finality to the proceedings and we want to stop spending resources on it. So the idea of having recourse to go back and reinstate the charges is kind of at that point --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So, in this case --

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: -- counterproductive.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: -- though, just confining it to this case, what's the disincentive to allowing an appeal here?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I mean, that is our whole concern, is that if you open the -- the floodgates to sort of a broad miscarriage-of- justice exception, there isn't really a powerful disincentive, and we will, in -- in various cases, be deprived the benefit of our bargain because we'll have to be litigating whether this exception, amorphous exception, to appeal waivers applies.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And you're -- you're asking --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: So I thought --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Your -- your --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: I thought you said that it wouldn't apply in particularly egregious cases. Did I mishear that?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: That's correct. I mean, our front-line position --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Correct that I didn't -- but --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct that you didn't miss --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Correct that I misheard it or that --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Our front-line position is that there should not be an exception for -- for even egregious miscarriage of justice because the Petitioner has argued this case based in contract doctrine, and we don't think there's a basis in contract doctrine for that. If this Court goes the route of wanting to have a -- a miscarriage exception for reasons other than contract law, maybe some kind of, you know, sort of federal common law policymaking or -- or constitutional, we would very strongly urge this Court to define whatever that exception would be very narrowly and to make clear it doesn't apply here.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: So all of the hypotheticals that Ms. Blatt presented us, you're comfortable defending all of those?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Our front-line position, yes, is that the appeal waivers in --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Well, you keep saying front-line position.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yeah. Sure.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: To me, that conveys the notion that you're ready to retreat.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: No, I -- I'm not -- I am not ready to retreat. I'm not ready to retreat. Our position is that the -- the appeal waiver is enforceable.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: So the order that you only -- only make your wife pregnant or what all the -- all the other examples, you say that's fine? We're --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes. Our position is that that's enforceable. And I recognize that sounds like a very harsh result, but I don't think it's a real-world concern that this --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: A -- a racist sentencing judge?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Again, our position is that it is enforceable. There are a few --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: A -- a sexist sentencing judge?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Someone who is biased against religious minorities?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes. The appeal waiver is enforceable.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: All of that -- all of that stands?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Because the -- the -- the point is that at the time that we're deciding whether to enforce the appeal waiver, all we have is a claim that there has been this particular type of error. And what the -- what the defendant has given up --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Why wouldn't that -- why wouldn't that bring --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- as part of the bargain is the right to bring that claim.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- the sort of disrepute on the federal judiciary that Judge Posner talked about, if you held a trial with 12 orangutans was his example, that that would have to be resolved. And I think we would all agree with that, right? But, here, you say any sentencing error of any kind.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So, to be clear, an appeal waiver is not an agreement to receive an unlawful sentence. You couldn't agree to receive trial by 12 orangutans. That would be an unlawful bargain. It is an agreement that the final decisionmaker --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Why isn't this an unlawful bargain?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: It's an agreement that the final decisionmaker on your sentence will be a district court rather than a court of appeals, and that is a bargain that the law tolerates.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: It's a district court who says I'm going to let an orangutan pick a sentence out of a hat?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Well, again, it -- it --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: And you would say, no, no right to appeal that, right?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: The -- the district court remains oath-bound. It is a bargain that at the time you're entering into it, all you are agreeing to is that the district court will be the final decisionmaker.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: That is not a bargain against the law. The law already tolerates the possibility of even egregious sentences going unreviewed because it has given defendants the choice whether to bring an appeal or not. If the law did not tolerate that, we would have a system of automatic appeals, as many states do, for example, for capital cases.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, can I -- you talk about opening the floodgates, but you're the one who's proposing a theory that no circuit has ever accepted. Yours is even more limited than the Fifth Circuit. You're saying only IAC as it relates to a knowing -- knowingly or not knowingly enter a plea is cognizable. The statutory maximum, you're saying, is not cognizable either unless the plea agreement tells you that the judge will sentence within the statutory maximum because the logic of your argument is, if I agree, if I say in the plea agreement the judge can sentence you any way he wants, that would not be cognizable, correct?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So -- so our position, yes, is that if in the plea agreement you expressly say I am waiving my right to appeal on the grounds that the statute -- that my sentence is above --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Now every other circuit has something broader than the Fifth Circuit. Virtually all of them have something for constitutional errors of the nature that Justice Gorsuch was call -- talking about either under a miscarriage-of-justice rubric or under a plain error or unconstitutional violation. So this is not floodgates that are opening. Whatever is being appealed now is pretty narrow. It -- it's a small fraction of the plea agreements are being appealed, and those that are, there's an even smaller fraction where there's any time being spent on them. So waiving a flag talking about floodgates opening seems rather exaggerated by the government.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So the burden that we experience in the circuits right now really depends on the way that this exception is described in the circuits. In the circuits that have open-ended exceptions for, you know, all constitutional claims, the U.S. attorney's offices do report that to be a significant burden because it's quite easy to characterize even garden-variety claims as a due process violation, as I think this case illustrates. In case -- in circuits that more carefully circumscribe their exceptions to two or three discrete situations, it is -- it is admittedly less of a burden, but the problem is that whether we get the benefit of our bargain, which was to not have to litigate an appeal, just turns entirely on the defendant's choice because, if the defendant chooses to raise a miscarriage-of-justice exception --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, if you don't -- if you're not happy with the bargain, do what she -- what you're threatening to do, which is go ahead and prosecute them.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: But, again, our -- our whole purpose of --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Your -- your -- your -- your --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- entering into the appeal waiver is that we didn't -- we wanted finality and we didn't want to have to keep litigating.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, but the problem with finality is that you're basing it on the good faith of a third actor, meaning you're -- you're depending on a district court judge to not -- or they are, a district court judge to follow their constitutional and statutory obligations. I would think it's unenforceable to enter a contract that says you are going to be sentenced by a judge who does not have to follow the law.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: That is -- that is very much not what an appeal waiver says.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: That's exactly what it says.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: An appeal waiver says --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, but that's -- that's exactly what you're saying.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Not at all, Your Honor. An appeal --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That's exactly what you're saying, which is, if the judge uses impermissible race factors, impermissible sex factors, religious factors, that can be waived. That's saying that a judge -- you're entering a contract that says, if your judge is biased, if he doesn't follow the Constitution, if he breaks plain -- if he makes plain error judgments that are clear and undisputed, there's no remedy for you.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So -- so, again, an appeal waiver is not saying you can be sentenced however you want by a judge. The judge is oath-bound. The judge has to follow the law all the time.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that's what you're saying.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: The -- the appeal waiver is just saying the final decisionmaker is the district court, and, by the way, that was the system that existed for a hundred years in this country. We didn't have sentence --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Ms. Jacoby --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: If that -- if that really is --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Ms. Jacoby --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Sorry.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- just out of curiosity, what's your back-line position?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Sure. So we -- we think -- we think that if this Court is going to go the miscarriage-of-justice route, again, we don't really see a basis for that in contract law, but if you're -- if you're going to go that route, our strong preference is that you follow the circuits that have, you know, basically done what the Fifth Circuit has done but just with one or two more exceptions that seem to capture some of the concerns that people have here. So we like, for example, the Seventh Circuit's test. I would put a little bit of a gloss on it for precision, but what I would say are the three circumstances that would be qualifying as -- as a miscarriage of justice would be a sentence that's explicitly based on a constitutionally impermissible factor like race; two, a sentence or condition that is categorically unlawful for the statutory offense of conviction; and, three, a sentence reached without even a minimum of civilized procedure, that's the example of, you know, the orangutan sentences you.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Why would it not be sufficient to say miscarriage of justice on the assumption that everybody who's going to be applying that standard knows it's a high bar, has seen it before, has thought about it before? The idea of, you know, defining it into three discrete situations is not usually what we do when we speak of miscarriages of justice, and yet, you know, everybody gets that it's a high bar.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: The problem with setting the high bar is not that we're concerned that -- that judges won't know what constitutes a miscarriage of justice. The problem is that defendants won't know and they may well think that their case does present the miscarriage of justice.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, maybe that's right.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: And then we have to litigate it, and that deprives us of the benefit of the bargain, which was to not have to litigate.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: You know, that -- that's right. But, if -- if that's the case, that kind of challenge can be easily dismissed. I mean, I guess I -- I wouldn't think that that would take very much of anybody's time.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: You know, we do end up having to brief the merits of these disputes because courts will sometimes just ask for more briefing. It -- the -- the circuits that have this more open-ended waiver -- pardon, open-ended exception, they do report a more serious burden to litigate it. So we do think that capturing the discrete kind of buckets of cases that do seem to give everyone concern, and I think those three do really capture the universe of what people are worried about, the, you know, categorically impermissible sentence or categorically impermissible condition, the sentenced by an orangutan, the race-based sentence --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- I think that is the universe, and so just limiting it to those three and making very clear what those exceptions are and then applying them to this case rather than signaling to lower courts and litigants that this is a borderline case, I -- I think would be far preferable.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Did you say ineffective assistance of counsel is one of them?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes, of course. I think we're all taking as given that ineffective assistance of counsel is one, and the Seventh Circuit does say that.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And -- and the sentence exceeding the statutory maximum?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So -- so I want to be careful about how I define that.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Because you used categorical rather than statutory, and I want to know what that --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes. So what I'm getting at with that is I think what everyone is worried about when they're talking about this sort of statutory maximum thing is the idea, like, you pleaded guilty to an offense that carries 10 years and you get sentenced to 15. So I'm trying to capture that. But what I'm trying to exclude are things that some courts have said are -- fall within the statutory maximum exception, which are things like whether your restitution was proximately caused by your conduct, whether certain prior offenses qualify as ACCA predicates. Courts have sometimes said that those fall within a statutory maximum exception on the idea that if they're correct about their claim, your sentence exceeds what Congress has lawfully authorized. And we think those types of claims are really what we have bargained to not have to be litigating. We have bargained that the final decisionmaker on those issues will be the district court.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What about supervised release conditions? So I asked Ms. Blatt this.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But supervised release conditions can be challenged on appeal notwithstanding an appeal waiver if?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: They are categorically unlawful for the statutory offense of -- of conviction, same as the sentence terms. So that falls in my second bucket as well.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What does categorically unlawful when you're applying it to supervised release conditions mean?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I know what it means when you're talking about the statutory --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- sentence.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I think that refers to the -- the type of condition that there's really never any justification for for this offense. I mean, like, the -- the -- a condition like you can never get pregnant during supervised release.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, what -- is there a term that -- because we have to write an opinion --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- that's going to capture this.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: That's why we think categorically unlawful is sort of the best that we can do. We don't want to have to be dealing --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That's kind of -- I don't think that captures it.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I mean, the issue is we don't want to have to be dealing with questions like, you know, was this sufficiently justified on the record, is there enough stuff in the record to support this, did the judge make enough fact findings? Those are exactly the kind of reasonableness issues that Petitioner says are out of bounds, and we don't want you to just be able to call it a due process claim and then all of a sudden we are doing those reasonable -- reasonableness inquiries when we have bargained not to. And, again, I want to be clear, when we're bargaining for this, this is to the defendant's advantage.
