I mean, there's a reason that the defense bar does not want to go the route that Justice Jackson proposed of getting rid of all of these altogether because this is something of great value to the government, and so it is a bargaining chip --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: No, I under --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- for the defendants.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But then the -- oh.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Sorry. Are you --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Go ahead.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I -- I appreciate that, but I guess what I'm a little surprised about with the government's hard-line position is that I can't quite figure out whether you are sticking with the contracts idea or not. You've said many, many times that this is about the bargain and the government is bargaining not to litigate and that's what we really have to care about. But, as Ms. Blatt points out, every contracts regime that I'm aware of has some exception where the court is looking at the fairness of the bargain, where there's some consideration of the, you know, different degrees of ability to negotiate, the fact that you might have people who are being coerced into accepting this kind of deal even though it's not knowing and voluntary. The court is policing that. And the government's position here seems to be that we are not supposed to be caring about that; we just need to focus on the fact that the government has made this deal, that it's not going to want to litigate any more of these cases. And so it kind of brings me to Justice Gorsuch's original point. Like, that seems kind of unconstitutional in a way that the primary value here is to prevent the government from having to litigate appeals when we're talking about people's constitutional rights. And so I'm just trying to understand, is it your position that notwithstanding the contracts-like nature of this endeavor, we are not to be treating this the way the courts ordinarily treat contracts, which is to consider these kinds of exceptions?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So I'm really glad you asked this question because I think this is a really important point for us. Our position is not that contract defenses do not apply to plea agreements or to appeal waivers. It is that the contract defenses that Petitioner has raised here fail. They fail because a subsequent sentence that a defendant receives under just ordinary principles of contract law, the ones that Petitioner has relied on here, do not make the agreement against public policy or unconscionable --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Right, but wouldn't you --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- or frustrated purpose.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- have to do that at an individual level? Like, I don't know how you could categorically say we allow for these defenses, but they're never going to apply in this situation because something like the fairness of an agreement really takes into account what's going on with respect to that particular case.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: This is why Justice Kagan is -- is -- is, I think, resisting the granular statement of what counts as a miscarriage of justice, because we could look at a particular case and say: This bargain, this scenario is unfair because it relates to the kinds of things that my colleagues have pointed out. Right?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So -- so, if we're in contract world, and I'm going to bracket the miscarriage-of-justice world, which I think, if this Court's going down that road, it's not on the basis of contract doctrine; it's -- it's something else, some other kind of --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Wait. We can -- we can fold --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- policymaking. But -- but --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: No, no, no. Let's fold the miscarriage-of-justice concept into something like public policy because it's a -- you agree that public policy is an exception, right, in -- under the contracts world? And one would think that if what is happening here is a miscarriage of justice, we -- we would say that that's against public policy. So let's put it under that umbrella. Go forward.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Sure. I'm happy to talk about public policy exception. Under contract doctrine -- again, I'm assuming we're in contract world. Under contract doctrine, the question is not whether the sentence is against public policy. The question is whether the agreement is against public policy.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: And the -- the agreement to waive your right to appeal is not against public policy. It's not against --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Because?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- the public policy of the appeal rights statute --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Because?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- because the policy of the appeal rights statute is one of defendant choice always. And it's not against the policy of the underlying, you know, say, constitutional amendment you say is violated. Take -- take, for example --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Ms. Jacoby --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: What about -- what about the Sentencing Reform Act, which says that appeals are important, that they incentivize the court and the parties to actually follow the law, follow the guidelines, not issue these egregious sentences, right? We have a congressional policy that, in fact, revamps the entire sentencing system to allow for appeals. So to suggest that an appeal waiver is not against public policy, I think, runs up against all of that.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: But, again, our system still, even after the Sentencing Reform Act, leaves it as a matter of defendant choice whether to exercise that appeal. If Congress really thought that the appeal --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: That's the question. That's the question today. That's my question that everybody else has given up --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- but I'm still working, right?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: If -- if --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: My question is, should it be left as defendant choice given all of these problems? Given the fact that it doesn't seem to advance anything other than the government's interest in not wanting to litigate, you know, these kinds of issues, should it be left to defendant choice in this circumstance?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: It -- it definitely should given that Congress has left it to defendant choice. I don't see a basis for this Court to upend that. And then, as to the idea that all we're doing here is protecting the government's interest in finality, no. I mean, this is a bargaining chip for defendants. The -- the appeal waiver is valuable for us, so, here, a deal with an appeal waiver was worth dropping nine charges for Petitioner. I have no idea what a deal without an appeal waiver would be worth, but --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Ms. -- Ms. --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- basic economic logic suggests it would be less. Different charges --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Ms. Jacoby, two --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- two questions on that. One is your answers to Justice Jackson about the contract approach, as you're calling it, you argue you win under contract principles. You don't argue that they don't apply.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct. Correct.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: So why doesn't this defendant get a remand --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Because we win --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- to argue contract?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- we win categorically. The subsequent sentence that the defendant receives --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah, but the -- if I --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- is never a basis to find that the contract is against public policy.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: If I -- if I might? The Fifth Circuit hasn't addressed that question. Nobody has addressed that question. This Court hasn't addressed that question. Why wouldn't we remand to allow the court of appeals to address that question?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I think what Petitioner has -- has asked this Court to do is to say that in some instances, an appeal waiver could be against public policy.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: And I'm asking the Court --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- to say it never could.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: No. No. What -- what -- what Petitioner is asking us to say is that the Fifth Circuit's rule that there are two and only two bases is not correct and that I should be allowed to try a contract defense. And you say you've got good contract defenses. God bless you. How about litigating them?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: To -- to make it a possibility that they try to raise a contract defense, I think Petitioner needs to show how any contract defense could in theory in the right case give relief here. And there is no public policy that an appeal waiver violates based on the subsequent sentence that --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: But, if we're not prepared to decide that in the first instance because no court has passed on that question, do you object to a remand to have a court --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: We very strongly object to a remand, particularly because of what it would signal about the facts of this case. You could say it may be possible in some case, but we would really urge you not to suggest that it would be this case because this is the type of appeal that even the circuits that have broad standards, I think, perhaps excepting the Ninth Circuit, would categorically not find this a miscarriage of justice.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: And -- and then --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: And if you suggest otherwise, that -- that truly will open the floodgates and cause a big problem for the United States.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Because -- because an issue that hasn't been litigated should never be litigated. Okay. All right. And -- and then you -- you -- you tout the benefits of these deals. Let's take this case as an example. The defendant here pled guilty to one count of stealing $32,000. He was sentenced as if he was guilty of all the counts the government dropped and a half a million dollars' worth of -- of stolen money. How -- if I think about this as an unconstitutional conditions possible problem, we normally say that there has to be some nexus between what you're giving up and what you get and some proportionality. And if he's being sentenced as if he committed all the crimes that weren't charged, it's pretty hard for me to see what benefit he's getting from an appeal waiver here. Can you help me with that?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Absolutely. So, first of all, on uncondition -- on unconstitutional conditions, there is no constitutional right to an appeal. So --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: There's a statutory right.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes, but --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: And -- well, as a matter of --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- it's not an unconstitutional condition and it doesn't burden your constitutional rights.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: As a matter of due process, you're entitled to that statutory right.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I mean, no, I think that you're just --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Really? You just --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- constitutionalizing the appeal right if you say that as a matter of due process you're entitled to the statutory right.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Oh, okay. Okay. All right. So --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: But -- but setting that aside, I think your second question is about --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: I -- I think maybe you better set that aside for my purposes at least.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Understood. Setting that aside, your second question, I think, is about if he got a good deal in the end here, if -- if, in fact, the sentence is -- is one that his bargain to drop nine charges proved to be a good deal. As -- as my friend has said, you know, he obviously thinks it ended up not being a good deal, but that is not a basis to undo a bargain and that's not a basis to find a bargain to be unconscionable. Unconscionability isn't about harsh results later when it proves you made a bad deal. Unconscionability is about a deal that you made that at the time you made it is so one-sided no rational --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: If --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- person would enter into it, and that was not this deal.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: If the government's position is a defendant can knowingly and voluntarily give up his appeal rights, I wonder what the government would say a defendant couldn't give up? Could a defendant give up, for example, his right to go to church after his conviction or to own a gun after his conviction --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- or his entitlement to federal unemployment benefits after his conviction?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- again, this comes back to my point that this is not -- an appeal waiver is not an agreement to be sentenced unlawfully. An agreement to be sentenced unlawfully would be against public policy --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Oh --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- because performance --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- okay. Okay. Now that's a --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- of the agreement would be against public policy. This is --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- that's an interesting test. Okay. So, if it's an unlawful sentence, he should be able to appeal?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Not at all.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: No?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: No, no, no, no, no. If the agreement is to receive an unlawful sentence, that is an agreement against public policy. But, here, the agreement is not to receive an unlawful sentence. The agreement still contemplates that the judge is oath-bound.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: It is an agreement that the final decisionmaker on your sentence is a --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Will --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- district court --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- will be --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- and that is not an unlawful agreement. No public policy forbids the district court from being the final decisionmaker on your -- on your -- on your case.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: All right. Go ahead.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: That was the status quo for a hundred years.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: What about -- what about --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Ms. Jacoby, can I -- can I ask you a question, Ms. Jacoby -- Ms. Jacoby? I'm having a hard time tracking exactly what the government's position is. So I asked Ms. Blatt, explain to me how these defenses that you're proposing fit within the contract analogy. And, I don't know, I -- I sense some going back and forth about whether the contract analogy is a perfect fit so that all these defenses are available. And, if so, I'm not really sure how the miscarriage-of-justice back-line position fits in, unless we try to shoehorn it through public policy. So explain to me what the government's theory is here.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Sure. So Petitioner has litigated this as a contract doctrines case, saying that all these contract defenses apply. Our -- our position is, if -- if we're going on contract defenses, no, none of them applies.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Would you go on contract -- well, let me -- let me ask you that just as a matter of first principles, is it the government's position that this should be litigated as a contract case?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I do think so. And I think that there's not really another source of law that Petitioner has identified to lodge this miscarriage-of-justice exception. It's not contract principles.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: No, no, no, no, the government. Like, do you think of this, do you think the Petitioner is right to litigate this? Because you've gone along with it --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes, yes.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: -- and you've said, if we're talking about contracts, it doesn't work. But do you think of this challenge as something to which the contract analogy should apply?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I do think that this type of argument is one to which the contract analogy should apply. There's no constitutional right to an appeal, so I don't think it makes sense to say that there is a constitutional right to an appeal in certain egregious cases, so the Constitution isn't really a home for this doctrine. And other than that, I think you would just be engaging in some kind of federal common law policymaking --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- where Congress hasn't --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So then --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- Ms. Jacoby --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So is the position -- oh, sorry. Can I just finish --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: No, go ahead.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: -- this one? So is your position that the contract analogy applies, but none of the contract defenses work in this context?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. So, when you're making this concession about, you know, potential miscarriage of justice, et cetera --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: -- you're just kind of pulling that from the ether?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: That -- so, there, we're assuming that you -- you are kind of going outside of contract doctrines and you are trying to find some sort of policy or maybe constitutional backstop, some safety valve. And we think, if you are going down that road, the safety valve I proposed to Justice Kagan is the right one, but, again, we do not think that that is the right way to go here.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Well, why isn't Justice Gorsuch right then? I mean, are we supposed to march through every single available contract defense for the Restatement and say this is why this couldn't apply here? This is why that couldn't apply here?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Petitioner has come up with four potential defenses. None of them applies. I -- I think that you can just categorically say --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: As to his case, but we're not --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: No, no, no, categorically, when a subsequent sentence -- sorry, a subsequent sentence does not make an appeal waiver against public policy. It doesn't make it unconscionable. It doesn't make it a breach of the implied duty of good faith. It doesn't frustrate the shared purpose that goes to the essence of the bargain. Just categorically those defenses all fail. If there are other defenses out there, it was on Petitioner to raise them, and he hasn't.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Is the --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Didn't argue that. Sorry.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice -- Justice Kagan?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Is -- is the premise of your answers to Justice Barrett that this Court has no power to establish rules intended to safeguard the integrity of the judiciary?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: That is not our position. But the rules need to be lodged, I think, in some source of law. There's no congressional source of law. There's no, really, constitutional source of law given that there is not a constitutional right to appeal. So I don't think it makes sense to say --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: No, I mean, that was my question. In other words, you're saying that we have to point to a statute, we have to point to a particular constitutional provision to set some boundary lines on what judges can do in a task that is supremely a judicial one.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I think that --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: That we can't just say, you know, there are some things that judges can do that would be a miscarriage of justice and we -- we -- we -- we don't need to point to a statute to be able to create a rule that polices the judiciary to that extent.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I think particularly because we are in the world of federal criminal law and federal criminal procedure, which is highly regulated by Congress and the Rules Committee, that's an especially, you know, dangerous place for this Court to be making policymaking, even though I understand that it's policymaking about what judges should do. It's policymaking about which court should be the last word on certain types of cases, and I think that really is the province of Congress and the Rules Committee. If you disagree with me and you are going down the road of -- of a miscarriage-of- justice exception, again, we strongly urge the Court to cabin it to the circumstances that really give everyone pause.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, whatever the miscarriage-of-justice exception says, whether it's less cabined, whether it's more cabined, you're saying that the only way that we can do that is by referring to contract law principles or by finding -- which I don't think exist because people would have found it -- some statute that allows us to do that?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Or constitutional principles or -- I -- I think you're maybe gesturing towards something to do with the supervisory powers of the courts or something like that. But this isn't really a good home for the supervisory powers because this isn't about supervising the conduct in the courtroom. This is really about making rules about when certain -- you know, when a decision should be final, whether that's at the district court stage or the court of appeals stage. That's kind of bread-and-butter federal criminal procedure stuff that Congress deals with and the Rules Committee deals with.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further? Justice Alito?

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, can't we say, to continue your colloquy with Justice Kagan, can we not say that a plea bargain has some attributes of a contract, but it's not like an ordinary contract. I don't know. Even the -- even the amici supporting Mr. Hunter say -- this is the Cato/ACLU brief, point 1-b, "plea bargaining is no ordinary contract negotiations." So we can adopt a principle that is informed by contract law without taking a position that you now seem to be taking, which is this is just like any other contract and all of the contractual defenses that might be recognized are available here. I find your position really hard to understand. You -- on the one hand, you say you don't want to be burdened with having to litigate a lot of -- a lot of appeals that are taken by defendants who have entered into a -- entered into an appeal waiver. And then you're -- you're happy to have judges decide -- don't you think that anything that -- any objection that a defendant might have could be -- could be characterized as unconscionable?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: If I -- if I --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: I mean, we occasionally see issues of unconscionability in cases involving -- involving arbitration. And, boy, some state courts really go to town on -- with the -- with the unconscionability argument. But you're happy to -- you're happy to deal with all those?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I'm -- I'm definitely happy to say why categorically appeal waivers are not unconscionable if -- if that's the question. If the question is whether there should be some -- some other rule that informs the court, yes, I agree plea bargains are not perfectly like contracts, so the -- the analogy doesn't hold in all respects. But you would have to say that there's a constitutional dimension. I mean, there would have to be sort of some source of law that is informing what rule the court makes if it's not a contract rule. And there's not really a constitutional principle at -- at play here, and I'm not really sure another source of law. So, no, I don't -- I want to be clear, we've been litigating this case as a contract case because that's how Petitioner has brought this. If the Court wants to say that there are aspects that are not like a contract and other rules should apply, it would just need to ground those -- those rules, I think, in something. I'm happy to explain, though, why.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Okay. Thanks.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, on the second question presented, there is a way of reading that transcript in which one could say the judge basically said, I'm going to impose a condition, come back to me later if you disagree. And then he mentions the right to appeal, that there was permission to appeal at least this -- this -- this condition. Why can't we read that colloquy as such?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Because a district court doesn't have authority to modify the parties' agreement, so --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, then that goes to the silence that was the government's position there. All right. What does that do to the ripeness question that Justice Barrett had? Presumably, you would argue at any point in time that the waiver applies, correct?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: It -- that depends on the language of the agreement. Here, this agreement doesn't bar Petitioner from seeking modification of the condition of supervised release. So he could do that. The Fifth Circuit has said that an appeal from the denial of modification is covered by a waiver if it's the type of agreement that -- pardon -- the type of appeal that could have been brought from the beginning if -- if --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That's -- isn't that the -- the vicious circle you're putting this defendant in, isn't it?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I -- I don't think we're putting the defendant in any circle.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The Fifth Circuit is.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I think the defendant entered into an agreement, and this was the terms of the deal.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And so you're saying tough luck because the Fifth Circuit won't let you do it, so we should let you do it? We should let you say --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: It is possible --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- it's not right?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- I think, under the terms of the Fifth Circuit's doctrine that if the type of appeal that he brought were more as applied and not the type of thing he really could have brought from the beginning, given that this appeal waiver doesn't cover --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Let's go --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- appeals from a modification --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- it could be allowed.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- you admitted that it would be unconscionable to contract to have a racist judge decide the case, correct?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I -- I admitted it would be against public policy to say my -- my sentence will be decided on the basis of race.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But it's not, in fact, against public policy to have a racist judge sentence you?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes, the sentence would be against public policy. But the question is whether the agreement is against public policy. And, again, the agreement is not to have --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But the -- but that's what you're -- you're --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- a racist sentence. The agreement is to have no appeal and that the agreement to have no appeal is not against any public policy.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Even though it involves, so -- a breach of the duty of good faith dealing, which applies to third parties that you delegate contract rights to.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: But a judge is not a delegee under -- under the agreement.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Oh, you can't have it both ways. He is either a judge acting in conformity with the law, or he is a delegee subject to the good faith and fair dealing condition. You want to pick and choose defenses.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Not at all, Your Honor. He is a judge acting in good faith in the law, but a presumption of the entire appeal waiver is that the defendant might not think that the sentence he ultimately receives is unlawful. That is kind of the core --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- premise.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- but it's not a question of unlawful because it's a question of whether he's violated statutory rights, whether he has used impermissible constitutional factors, whether he's violated statutory limitations, imposing restitution when the statute doesn't provide for it and the agreement doesn't contemplate it. He's acted outside the bounds of expectation.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: The relevant expectation under contract doctrine -- again, I'm -- I'm assuming now we're in contract world. The relevant expectations are not the parties' expectations as to what sentencing outcome will occur. Contracts hold even when --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I -- I agree. I submit things --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- unforeseen and unexpected things happen. The relevant expectation is what -- how the parties expected their agreement would operate, whatever the outcome at sentencing.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So why isn't it -- and going back to Justice Kagan's question -- we all assume that judges will act lawfully, not that they can't make errors in calculations but that they'll act lawfully and not unconstitutionally?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: We -- we do assume that, but the premise of the agreement here is that both sides might not think that the judge has acted lawfully, and if the defendant thinks that the judge hasn't acted lawfully, he can't appeal. That's kind of the whole premise of this agreement here. That is the assumption.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. Then we're going around in the circle we started with.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch, anything further?

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: You agree that some prospective waivers are just unenforceable, so, for example, you cannot prospectively waive your Title VII rights?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct. Yes. This is quite different, though. I mean, you can't prospectively waive your right to sue under Title VII, this Court has said, because the right to sue is basically integral to the substantive policy of Title VII. But the right to appeal is not integral to the substantive policy of any constitutional amendment or statutory amendment or anything like that.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Except for Congress --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: And we know that because it didn't --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- granted a right to appeal, just as it granted a right to pursue Title VII remedies.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Waiving the right to appeal also isn't against the policy of the right to appeal because that would just mean we could never have any statutory waivers.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, we didn't have appeal waivers until about three minutes ago in Missouri.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: We also didn't have appeals for a hundred years, respectfully.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah. And we also --

turns.name: JACOB
turns.text: We didn't have sentencing appeals at all.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: And we also didn't have plea agreements before that if you really want to go back, counsel. So I'm not sure that works for you. And then, with respect to contract in what you call contract world, you agree that at least in principle, a defendant would have contract defenses, correct?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Let's see. I appreciate your having alternative arguments and a party having alternative arguments. I think that's helpful because you don't know where the nine of us are going to go. But then it's important to keep -- for me, to keep clear on what the alternatives are. Your first one's the contract, no exceptions. Okay. The lower courts have not gone that way, have rejected it. Assume --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- I'm not going that way.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay? Just me.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay? So first alternative out for me. On the second alternative, I think you raise a good point, what source of law are we doing, but all the lower courts have been doing it, right? All the courts of appeals. And I -- could be federal common law, could be supervisory power, but it's there. And I think I'm just taking that as a given, and that is --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yeah, courts have done it. The way this has arisen is courts, you know, in the early 2000s, late '90s, often confronted with this would say, yes, these are okay, but, of course, we would never enforce it in X, Y, Z circumstances without really explaining why.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: It -- it -- but it's -- but there's a --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: But, yes, they have said that. They have said that.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: At this point, I -- I take your technical point and maybe it's more than technical, where does that come from, but it's -- it's everywhere in the courts of appeals. My old court, all -- every court, right? And it might be interpreting the federal criminal rule, it might be supervisory power, it might be federal common law, it might be going to what an appeal is in the statute, to Justice Gorsuch's point, but it's there. Then it's just what are the exceptions, right?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay. So, if we're on that, it's just what are the exceptions and whether to identify a general miscarriage of justice or to list three or four and then to say they're not exclusive, correct?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And there's a pretty good consensus of exceptions in most of the courts of appeals.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct. Correct. And the ones that we've articulated I think track the --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Like, in -- in other words, I think this is not so hard in the sense that there's a kind of defined list. What happened here is the list is shorter than what most of the courts of appeals have, correct?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: If this Court is going down that road, I think saying there's still a defined list; there's just, you know, two more bullets on the list --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Exactly. Exactly.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- we can live with that as long as the last bullet isn't catch-all, know it when you see it if it's sufficiently egregious, because that is a real problem for us. If the bullets are just -- if you just say the Fifth Circuit has one too few bullets, two too few bullets, that makes sense. But, again, we don't think there are any bullets that could capture Petitioner's argument.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, in the D.C. Circuit, we did have a bullet that said results in a miscarriage of justice --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- and I don't remember the place falling apart.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So I do think that if this Court were to announce a standard like that, we would see kind of a flood of that.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: And there has also at this point been kind of enough common law, I guess, you know, built up around it that courts have denied those miscarriages enough that defendants may raise them less. But, if this Court were to announce that standard, we think defendants would raise them a lot. And, again, I'm not suggesting courts would find miscarriages of justice constantly. The opposite. But that's the very problem.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But it's going to come up.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I mean, if we're looking for the needle in a haystack, but in every case, we're being deprived the benefit of our bargain, that's the problem.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And the ultimate benefit is the resource, right?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct. I mean, we enter into 45,000 plea agreements a year, so --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And, ultimately, it's a judge-protective thing too because the judges are -- yeah.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I got -- got all that. The amicus brief of the Association of -- of -- let's see, which one -- the Association of Federal Defenders says, if we are too strict on this, there are going to be fewer plea bargains, so the resource problem is actually going to be worse. Do you want to respond to that very quickly? Because I'm using time.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I -- I don't agree with that. I mean, the most common alternative to entering into a guilty plea pursuant to an agreement is not a trial; it's entering into a guilty plea without an agreement, where the United States gives no concessions and the defendant retains his right to appeal his sentence. That's, I think, kind of the most common alternative that we have now and that's, I think, where things would get channeled. So I think we'll still have pleas, but those pleas will probably be less favorable to defendants because we're not giving the concessions.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Counsel, would the government be amenable to having defense counsel insert qualifications on the right to appeal and bargaining for the kinds of exceptions that some of the courts of appeals currently allow?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes. I'm -- I'm very glad you raised that point. Defendants are always free to do that. I mean, again, I -- I want to be clear it's a very important term to us, so in some cases, we won't budge, and in many cases, we won't budge. But it's case by case, it's district by district, and we do enter into plea agreements with carveouts for constitutional claims, carveouts that limit the appeal waiver to only certain guidelines ranges, all the rest. And we have in the appropriate cases entered into plea agreements with no sentencing appeal waiver at all.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So it really is a matter of defendant choice here.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So you've emphasized that if we're going to do something other than the contract analogy, we have to find some source of law in which to ground it, and you've been pressed about supervisory power. I'm wondering whether, in addition to supervisory power, another rationale is that this isn't a pure contract agreement because it has to be accepted by a third party. The district judge has a role in the process. And, hence, because the district judge has a role in the process, there have to be some parameters to what the district court does in that process. And so our supervisory power over -- it's not just purely between the government and the defendant. So our supervisory power over the third party in that process is a source of law that can justify some of these exceptions.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I see that argument. I think that could make some sense. I -- I mean, I want to be clear, it's often the same judge that approves the plea deal as the one who sentences. It's not necessarily, but if you're just sort of saying the district court, not specific judge, I -- I -- I -- I see that point. And if that's how this Court wants to ground a series of exceptions, I -- I think that makes sense. I -- I want to be clear, we're -- we're -- we have gone sort of hard on the contract doctrine here because that's how Petitioner briefed this case, but I'm not trying to suggest that this Court lacks powers to --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But you would prefer -- I guess I'm still a little bit confused. When I asked you that before, like, are you only talking about it in terms of contract because the Petitioner did, you said no, you do think of it as a contract.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Because there's no constitutional right to appeal and because Petitioner --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- hasn't briefed it as a supervisory power or on this theory that you've suggested, we do think the -- the most logical place would be if -- if the -- the avenue that you've suggested is -- is the power to supervise the accepting of the plea, I mean, I -- I think that that could make some sense as an avenue to accept these carveouts.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. Thanks.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Jackson?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I just want to clarify one factual point that I thought you were exploring with Justice Barrett. Are appeal waivers required in every district across this country? Aren't there some places -- my understanding was that this is not uniform.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So, I mean, they're never required. It's a term of a voluntary contract.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: No, I understand. But aren't there -- aren't there districts in which the background principle is not to put an appeal waiver in plea agreements?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: So I'm actually not sure if there are any U.S. Attorney's Offices that have a stock agreement, a stock plea agreement without an appeal waiver, but some are more willing to bargain to --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Yup. Good.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: -- to bargain it away than others.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: All right. And one other thing. You have repeatedly honed in on the nature of the appeal waiver. You say an appeal waiver is an agreement to let the court be the final arbiter at sentencing. Is that what the government's position is?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: What I'm wondering is whether that same position would hold with respect to an agreement pertaining to the trial. So let me give you a hypothetical. Suppose we have a defendant who wants to go to trial, but he'd like to lessen his potential sentencing exposure in the event that he's found guilty. Could he contract with the government to prospectively waive his right to appeal any and all trial errors, errors, in other words, we're leaving it to the trial court to make all the calls with respect to evidence, et cetera, and I'm not going to as a matter of contract appeal any of those errors?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I'm -- I'm not prepared to commit on all trial errors, but, of course, you can prospectively waive right to, for example, certain evidentiary objections. That was Mezzanatto.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Why wouldn't you commit it's the same principle?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: If -- if -- if the idea is that we can contract around making the trial judge be the final arbiter, which is what I understood you to have said --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I see what you're saying. You're saying that there would just be no appeal, like, from the trial. Yes, I would think that would be okay.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And you would think that there would be no public policy interest of the court or the public in ensuring that people get a fair trial?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Again, I just -- I -- I'm really wary to get out ahead when we're talking about these trial issues which are outside the scope of this case.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: No, I'm not -- I'm not asking you to get out ahead. I'm testing your principle that --

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- there is no -- I'm testing your principle that there is no problem with an agreement to prospectively appeal errors in a proceeding that will ultimately impact whether it's the trial or the sentencing. You've said the reason why that's not a problem at sentencing is because the parties can agree to make the sentencing judge the final arbiter. So, if he makes a mistake or not, he believes that's the right answer, and you're not going to appeal that. All I'm doing is transporting that same principle to the trial, and your concerns about it are precisely the concerns that I have with respect to the sentencing issue.

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I just don't know if there are issues at trial that are not waivable due to some external public policy that doesn't apply at sentencing.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Not the issue. The ability to appeal it. That's the point you keep making with respect to the sentence. So, regardless of how fundamental the error is, you say at sentencing, we can agree that the trial judge is going to make the final call or the sentencing judge. And all I'm asking you is couldn't you, under your logic, do the same thing with respect to trials and wouldn't that be a problem from the standpoint of ensuring that people actually get a fair trial as a matter of public policy?

turns.name: JACOBY
turns.text: I -- I don't think so, again, given that we didn't have the right to criminal appeals for a hundred years.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Rebuttal, Ms. Blatt.

turns.name: BLATT
turns.text: Thank you. Number one, just some data points. 1992 was the first court of appeals that said defendants can't be deemed to have sentenced at the -- entirely at the whim of the discretion of the district court. So that's Fourth Circuit. 2009, D.C. Circuit was the last circuit to recognize exceptions. So all 11 circuits have weighed in, and I don't see the government running out of resources. In terms of authority, they're completely separate doctrines. One is the contract doctrine and one is miscarriage of justice. In terms of the authority, it does, I think, come from an inherent equitable power. You could think of it as the government trying to seek specific performance. You could think of it as supervisory or inherent or, in terms of separation of powers, we're not going to let sentences bring the judiciary into disrepute. And this would be a sea change. Number two, all the cases that I mentioned were not hypotheticals. They were all real on pages in our reply brief and our opening brief about the castration, et cetera. The Fourth Circuit case cited by Cato is a case where the district court 20 times missentenced the defendant knowing his conduct would be shielded by the appeal waiver. So this is a concern. Number five, on remand, in terms of why we should get a remand, we haven't even briefed the unconstitutionality of this. The government says this is silly, but they've never asserted a justification for the mandatory medication condition. They've never even said why it's there. If they're really worried about resources, how about just confessing error and let the defendant just serve out his supervised release. It's not that complicated. Number five, in terms of the contract, how it is as a matter of law not unconscionable or frustration of purpose, this is hornbook contract law. Billions of contracts have open-ended terms. Quantities, discretion of a party, those are things that the parties don't yet know is going to happen. So this happens all the time. You have an open-ended contract. That's what an appeal waiver is. Also, I forgot counting how many times the government kept saying benefit of the bargain, deal, agreement. And all we're saying, it is beyond hypocritical to say the government is entitled to enforcement, yet as -- and using contract law. They literally brief their cases using contract law. It is inconceivable and hypocritical and embarrassing to say a criminal defendant has no contract defense at least when the government is seeking to enforce the contract. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
